blurb: Fluffy, thread-like flower clusters in soft powder-blue, lavender, and white smother neat mounding plants from early summer to frost — a long-blooming bedding annual butterflies and bees work all season.
water: Moderate; keep evenly moist — wilts quickly if it dries out
seasons: Mounded green foliage spring; fuzzy blue-purple-white flower clusters early summer to frost; killed to the ground by first hard frost
wildlife: Long-season nectar for butterflies, bees, and other pollinators
toxic: Poisonous — contains hepatotoxic pyrrolizidine alkaloids; not for eating, dangerous to grazing livestock
habitat: Moist disturbed ground, fields & forest edges

care.hardiness: A frost-tender annual grown for a single Front Range season — it is killed by the first hard fall frost, so no winter hardiness applies. It is easy and dependable as a warm-season bedder here and is simply replanted each spring.[1][2]
care.planting: Set out nursery transplants (or your own seedlings) after the mid-May average last frost, once nights stay warm — it resents cold soil and any late frost. In containers and window boxes it excels: use a quality moist-but-well-drained potting mix and water often, since pots dry fast and ageratum wilts the moment its roots go dry. There is no overwintering — it is a one-season plant.[1][2]
care.sun: Full sun for the heaviest bloom, but in Boulder's intense high-altitude summer light a little afternoon shade keeps it fresher and reduces wilting; deep shade thins the flowering.[1][2]
care.soil: Average to rich, organic, consistently moist but well-drained soil; near-neutral pH suits it. It does not tolerate dry, baked soil — mulch helps hold moisture.[1][2]
care.water: Moderate and steady — keep the soil evenly moist. Plants wilt quickly when soil dries, so don't let them parch; at the same time avoid waterlogged ground, which invites root rot.[1][2]
care.spacing: Space dwarf bedding types about 6 in apart and taller cutting types about 12 in apart for a solid mounded edge.[2]
care.propagation: Grown from seed (it is an annual). The seed is tiny — surface-sow and press in, do not bury, as it needs light to germinate; start indoors in late winter (about 6–8 weeks before last frost) or direct-sow after frost. Pinch young plants once to encourage branching.[1][2]
care.sow: Surface-sow the dust-fine seed and barely press it in — light aids germination; start indoors in late winter or direct-sow after the mid-May last frost.[1]
care.depth: Do not cover the seed (or cover only the barest dusting) — it needs light to germinate.[1]
care.bloom: Fluffy flower clusters from early summer to frost (roughly June–October here); shearing or deadhead-tidying spent clusters keeps the display fuller and neater.[1][2]
care.feeding: A light feeding of balanced fertilizer keeps container plants blooming; in decent garden soil little feeding is needed.[2]
care.maintenance: Shear lightly midseason if plants get leggy to renew compact bloom; otherwise low-maintenance. Pull and compost after the first hard frost kills it.[1][2]
care.troubles: Powdery mildew is the main issue in hot, humid, or crowded plantings with poor air circulation; whiteflies, aphids, and spider mites can appear, and root rot strikes in soggy soil. Good spacing, airflow, and even (not waterlogged) moisture prevent most problems.[1][2]

edible.summary: Poisonous — Ageratum houstonianum is an ornamental, not a food; it contains hepatotoxic pyrrolizidine alkaloids. Do not eat any part.[3][4]
edible.caution: All parts contain pyrrolizidine alkaloids (and precocenes), liver toxins that cause cumulative, irreversible hepatotoxicity — and the plant is a documented livestock poison, with cattle, horses, and pigs most susceptible to liver damage from grazing it. It has also been linked to photosensitization. There is no safe edible preparation: never eat it, keep it out of pasture and away from grazing animals, and wash hands after handling. Grow it strictly as an ornamental pollinator plant.[3][4]
